skill.name: m365-agents-py
skill.description: Build Python agents for Microsoft 365, Teams, and Copilot Studio using the Microsoft Agents SDK, aiohttp hosting, routing, streaming responses, OAuth, and MSAL authentication.
skill.summary: Create Microsoft 365 and Teams agents with the Python Microsoft Agents SDK.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings mostly come from Markdown code fences, Python import examples, and documented setup commands rather than hidden executable skill behavior. The skill is legitimate Microsoft 365 agent guidance, but it has medium risk because examples load secrets from environment variables, acquire OAuth tokens, and make authenticated outbound calls to Microsoft services.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated outbound Microsoft service calls
medium_findings.description: The examples acquire tokens and send authenticated requests to Microsoft Graph, Azure OpenAI, and Copilot Studio endpoints. This is expected for the skill purpose, but misuse could expose user profile data or tenant resources if scopes and consent are not controlled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly demonstrates token acquisition and authenticated network calls. The endpoints are Microsoft services and match the skill purpose, so this is a legitimate but sensitive pattern.

medium_findings.title: Environment-based secret handling
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to place client secrets, tenant IDs, Azure OpenAI endpoints, and API keys in environment configuration and load them at runtime. This is a standard pattern, but secrets must be protected and never committed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable names include client secrets and API keys, and load_dotenv is used. The documentation also tells users to keep secrets out of source code, reducing malicious concern.

low_findings.title: Static import and Markdown execution false positives
low_findings.description: The dynamic import and Ruby backtick findings are scanner artifacts from Python import blocks, Markdown inline code, and fenced examples. No evidence found that the skill itself executes hidden commands or dynamically imports untrusted code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are Markdown formatting or ordinary Python imports and best-practice text. They do not show shell backtick execution, eval, or dynamic loading from user input.

low_findings.title: Filesystem pattern false positives
low_findings.description: The path traversal and hidden file warnings do not correspond to path traversal logic in the skill content. The flagged context is documentation and API scope text, not filesystem reads or writes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain a newline in a status message and a Microsoft Power Platform scope ending in .default. No file path construction or traversal sink is present.

low_findings.title: No prompt injection evidence found
low_findings.description: The reviewed skill content does not contain instructions that claim to override system policy, skip security analysis, or impersonate higher-priority instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file was reviewed for suspicious instruction language. No prompt injection attempt was found in the visible skill content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer token use in HTTP headers
dangerous_patterns.description: The Graph API example sends an OAuth bearer token in an Authorization header. This is normal for Graph calls, but logs, errors, and debugging output must avoid exposing tokens.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The bearer token pattern is explicit and security-sensitive. It targets Microsoft Graph and is consistent with OAuth guidance rather than exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local dotenv configuration loading
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples load local environment configuration for SDK setup. This can read sensitive local values, so users should use secret managers or protected deployment configuration for production.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly documents .env-style settings and calls load_dotenv. The pattern is common, but it requires secret handling discipline.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dependency installation commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The installation section asks users to install Microsoft Agents SDK packages and related dependencies. This is expected setup work, but users should verify package names and versions before running installation commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plain pip install instructions for packages named in the skill metadata. The risk is supply-chain exposure, not hidden command execution.

content.value_statement: Teams and Copilot Studio agents need reliable Python patterns for routing, authentication, and streaming. This skill provides practical Microsoft Agents SDK examples for hosted agents, OAuth flows, Graph calls, and Copilot Studio clients.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install Microsoft Agents SDK Python packages and aiohttp dependencies.
Defines an aiohttp-hosted AgentApplication with CloudAdapter and JWT middleware.
Demonstrates message, conversation update, invoke, fallback, and error handlers.
Shows OAuth-protected routes that retrieve tokens and call Microsoft Graph.
Demonstrates Azure OpenAI streaming responses with sensitivity metadata.
Includes a Copilot Studio client flow with MSAL token acquisition.
limitations: It is documentation and example guidance, not a complete production application.
It requires current Microsoft package versions and API signatures to be verified before use.
It does not implement persistent production storage or a complete token cache.
It assumes users already have valid Microsoft tenant, app, and service configuration.

use_cases.title: Create a Teams Agent Prototype
use_cases.description: Use the hosted AgentApplication example to create a local Teams or Microsoft 365 agent endpoint.

use_cases.title: Add OAuth-Protected Commands
use_cases.description: Use the auth handler examples to add commands that request user consent and call Microsoft Graph.

use_cases.title: Test Copilot Studio Integration
use_cases.description: Use the Copilot client pattern to start conversations and send questions to a configured agent.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline a minimal Python Microsoft 365 agent with aiohttp hosting and a message handler.

prompt_templates.title: Add Secure Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to list the environment variables I need for a Microsoft Agents SDK Python project and explain which values are sensitive.

prompt_templates.title: Design Authenticated Graph Commands
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a Teams agent command that signs in a user, gets a Graph token, and returns basic profile information.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Production Hardening
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my Microsoft 365 Python agent design for storage, token handling, streaming cleanup, and deployment risks.

output_examples.input: Create a starter architecture for a Microsoft 365 Python agent.
output_examples.output: A concise architecture covering aiohttp hosting, CloudAdapter setup, AgentApplication routing, JWT middleware, environment configuration, and a local messages endpoint.

output_examples.input: Explain how to add a signed-in Microsoft Graph profile command.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step plan for configuring an OAuth handler, requesting a token, calling the profile endpoint, handling missing consent, and avoiding token exposure.

output_examples.input: Review my agent before production deployment.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering package versions, secret storage, persistent state, token cache design, Graph permission scope, streaming cleanup, and tenant configuration.

best_practices: Verify Microsoft Agents SDK package versions and import paths before implementation.
Store secrets in protected deployment configuration and exclude local environment files from source control.
Use persistent storage, strict OAuth scopes, and token-safe logging for production agents.
anti_patterns: Do not commit client secrets, tenant configuration, API keys, or local environment files.
Do not use in-memory storage for production conversations or user authorization state.
Do not request broad Graph permissions when a command only needs narrow profile or message access.

faq.question: Does this skill create a complete Microsoft 365 agent?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation patterns and examples that developers adapt into a full project.

faq.question: Which Python packages does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Microsoft Agents SDK packages for hosting, aiohttp integration, activity handling, MSAL authentication, and Copilot Studio.

faq.question: Can it help with Teams agent routing?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows conversation update handlers, message handlers, invoke handlers, fallback routing, and error handling.

faq.question: Does it cover authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes MSAL configuration, OAuth handler usage, token retrieval, sign-out, and Microsoft Graph request patterns.

faq.question: Is it safe to use in production as written?
faq.answer: No. The examples need production storage, protected secret management, version verification, logging review, and complete token cache design.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
